Description: Break UI into small, reusable components
Do: One component per file, single responsibility, clear props interface
Don't: Don't create monolithic components with multiple responsibilities
Code Good:
function UserCard({ user }) { return
; }'

Description: Design clear, minimal prop interfaces
Do: Use primitive props, avoid passing objects when possible
Don't: Don't pass entire objects as props

Description: Keep state as close to where it's used as possible
Do: Lift state only when multiple components need it
Don't: Don't lift state to parent when only one child needs it

Description: Choose appropriate state management for complexity
Do: Use useState for local, useReducer for complex, Context for shared
Don't: Don't use Context for frequently changing values

Description: Always specify correct dependencies in useEffect
Do: Include all used state/props in dependency array
Don't: Don't use empty dependency array when effect depends on values

Description: Clean up side effects in useEffect return
Do: Return cleanup function for subscriptions, timers, event listeners
Don't: Don't forget to clean up to prevent memory leaks
Code Good: useEffect(() => { const sub = subscribe(id); return () => sub.unsubscribe(); }

Description: Use React.memo, useMemo, useCallback for expensive operations
Do: Memoize expensive computations and stable callbacks
Don't: Don't memoize cheap calculations or primitive values

Description: Split code at route level using React.lazy
Do: Lazy load route components, use Suspense for loading states
Don't: Don't import all routes statically

Description: Virtualize long lists to improve performance
Do: Use react-window or react-virtuoso for large lists
Don't: Don't render thousands of DOM nodes

Description: Use controlled components or form libraries
Do: Control inputs with useState, or use React Hook Form
Don't: Don't use uncontrolled inputs without validation

Description: Implement client-side validation with clear feedback
Do: Use form libraries with built-in validation, show inline errors
Don't: Don't only validate on submit

Description: Use error boundaries to catch rendering errors
Do: Create error boundary components, show fallback UI
Don't: Don't let errors crash the entire app
Code Good: class ErrorBoundary extends React.Component { componentDidCatch(error) { this.setState({ hasError: true }); } render() { if (this.state.hasError) return <FallbackUI />; return this.props.children; } }'

Description: Handle errors in async operations (fetch, promises)
Do: Use try/catch, error states, retry mechanisms
Don't: Don't ignore promise rejections
Code Good: try { const data = await fetch(url); } catch (error) { setError(error.message); }'

Description: Extract reusable logic into custom hooks
Do: Prefix with 'use', return specific values, keep focused
Don't: Don't create hooks that do too many things

Description: Follow React's rules of hooks
Do: Call hooks at top level, only from React functions
Don't: Don't call hooks conditionally or in loops

Description: Minimize bundle size with tree shaking
Do: Import specific functions, use dynamic imports
Don't: Don't import entire libraries

Description: Use TypeScript for type safety
Do: Define interfaces for props, state, API responses
Don't: Don't use 'any' type or skip type annotations
Code Good: interface UserProps { name: string; email: string; onClick: () => void; }'

Description: Write tests for component behavior
Do: Test user interactions, not implementation details
Don't: Don't test internal state or implementation

Description: Use composition over inheritance for component flexibility
Do: Pass components as props or use children
Don't: Don't create complex prop types for component variations

Description: Choose controlled or uncontrolled based on needs
Do: Use controlled for validation/sync, uncontrolled for simple forms
Don't: Don't mix controlled and uncontrolled in same form
